content:
We're working on an LFX Mentorship assignment: design an architecture that adds
NVIDIA DPU support to the OPI (Open Programmable Infrastructure) DPU operator by
maximizing reuse of NVIDIA's existing DOCA Platform Framework (DPF) operator,
rather than reimplementing DPU lifecycle management from scratch. I'll send this
as a series of prompts ‚Äî treat this first one as research only. Do not design
anything yet.

Here is ground truth I've already verified by hand (as of mid-2026) ‚Äî use it as a
starting baseline, but re-confirm anything version-specific yourself, since both
projects move fast:

OPI DPU operator (opiproject/dpu-operator upstream; mirrored downstream as
openshift/dpu-operator):
- A standard Kubebuilder / controller-runtime Kubernetes Operator: a
  dpu-operator-controller-manager Deployment reconciling CRDs.
- A singleton DpuOperatorConfig custom resource is the top-level switch; creating
  it rolls out a per-node dpu-daemon DaemonSet plus vendor-specific "vsp"
  (Vendor Specific Plugin) pods.
- Intel and Marvell each ship their own VSP container/daemon (Intel VSP, Intel P4
  VSP, Marvell VSP + a separate Marvell control-plane agent) implementing a
  common internal contract (see the dpu-api directory) that the vendor-neutral
  daemon calls into. This is the existing per-vendor extension point.
- An optional ServiceFunctionChain CR deploys a workload pod onto the DPU via a
  dedicated dpu-cni.
- Supports both a 1-cluster topology (host and DPU nodes in the same cluster)
  and a 2-cluster topology (separate host cluster and DPU-side cluster).
- A network-resources-injector webhook wires DPU-backed network resources into
  pods.

NVIDIA DPF (github.com/NVIDIA/doca-platform):
- Also a controller-runtime/Kubebuilder-style operator, installed via Helm as
  dpf-operator into a dpf-operator-system namespace, configured by a singleton
  DPFOperatorConfig (API group operator.dpu.nvidia.com/v1alpha1).
- Splits the world into a Host Cluster (runs the DPF operator, provisions the
  physical DPUs) and a DPU Cluster (a separate Kubernetes control plane ‚Äî backed
  by the Kamaji virtual-control-plane project, or a pre-existing "static"
  cluster referenced via a kubeconfig Secret ‚Äî that the DPUs join as worker
  nodes and that runs the services deployed onto them).
- Provisioning objects (provisioning.dpu.nvidia.com/v1alpha1): BFB (BlueField
  Bootstream OS image), DPUFlavor (firmware/OS config template), DPUSet
  (provisions DPU objects across selected nodes, like a MachineSet), DPU (one
  physical DPU ‚Äî its controller drives a per-node DMS pod plus a Host Network
  Configuration daemon through OS install, reboot, VF/bridge setup, and a
  kubeadm join), DPUCluster (the DPU-side control plane itself).
- Service objects (svc.dpu.nvidia.com/v1alpha1): DPUService (deploys a Helm
  chart onto the DPU cluster ‚Äî this is how vendors package DOCA apps),
  DPUServiceChain / DPUServiceInterface / DPUServiceNAD (service-function
  chaining and secondary-network wiring), DPUServiceIPAM,
  DPUServiceCredentialRequest (cross-cluster credentials). DPUDeployment is the
  documented, recommended higher-level object bundling a DPUSet with a set of
  DPUServices and their chaining into one lifecycle-managed unit.
- Ships a status CLI, dpfctl, that prints a kubectl-tree-style view across all
  of the above.

Notice the structural parallel: OPI's 1-cluster/2-cluster host+DPU split and
DPF's Host Cluster/DPU Cluster split are the same underlying idea, arrived at
independently.

Your job this turn:
1. Pull the current state of opiproject/dpu-operator, openshift/dpu-operator,
   and NVIDIA/doca-platform yourself (clone, gh, or fetch ‚Äî whatever you have),
   and skim READMEs, api/ or docs/architecture folders, and CRD manifests. Work
   from the current code, not just my summary.
2. Confirm or correct every bullet above against what you actually find. Flag
   anything that's changed.
3. List every extension point OPI's operator currently exposes for adding a new
   vendor ‚Äî is the VSP contract the only one, or are there others (webhooks,
   additional CRDs, a plugin registry)?
4. List every place in DPF clearly designed to be driven by an external system
   rather than a human (CRDs meant to be created programmatically, status
   conditions meant to be watched, the "static cluster" bring-your-own-cluster
   hook).
5. Write a requirements/constraints summary under 500 words: what must the
   integration preserve on the OPI side (existing API surface, no regression
   for Intel/Marvell), what it can lean on DPF to solve so we don't rebuild it,
   and what's explicitly out of scope for this exercise.

Don't propose an architecture yet.

Research complete. I pulled current `main` of all three repos, deep-read them with three parallel agents, and spot-verified every high-stakes correction firsthand. Full notes are persisted in [research/00-baseline-research.md](research/00-baseline-research.md) and [research/01-requirements-constraints.md](research/01-requirements-constraints.md). **No architecture proposed** ‚Äî this is research only.

## 0. Baseline (as of 2026-07-03)

| Repo | HEAD | Date |
|---|---|---|
| opiproject/dpu-operator | `3092bcb` (PR #1 `upstream-k8s-portability`) | 2026-04-28 |
| openshift/dpu-operator | `846e112` (PR #636 `dpunetwork-controller`) | 2026-05-07 |
| NVIDIA/doca-platform | `234e7d4` (DPUFlavorTemplate render) | 2026-07-02 |

**Biggest lineage surprise:** despite the org names, **openshift/dpu-operator is the active/mature line** and **opiproject/dpu-operator is a freshly-reorganized *portability fork* of it that lags** ‚Äî 4 CRDs vs 5 (no `DpuNetwork`), older `dpu-api/api.proto`. Both `PROJECT` files literally say `repo: github.com/openshift/dpu-operator`. **Treat openshift/dpu-operator as the integration target.**

## 1‚Äì2. Confirming / correcting your bullets

**OPI operator** ‚Äî mostly right, four corrections worth flagging:

- ‚úÖ Kubebuilder operator; Deployment resolves to `dpu-operator-controller-manager`.
- ‚öÝÔ∏è **CRDs are in group `config.openshift.io/v1`, not a custom OPI group.** `DpuOperatorConfig` is **cluster-scoped**, singleton enforced by a *validating webhook* (name must be `dpu-operator-config`).
- ‚öÝÔ∏è **The "create config ‚Üí VSP pods" chain has an extra hop.** Creating the config rolls out only the `dpu-daemon` DaemonSet (+ NRI webhook + NADs). Each daemon then **autodetects hardware ‚Üí creates a `DataProcessingUnit` CR ‚Üí a separate controller renders the VSP pod**. So there are really **~5 CRDs**, and `DataProcessingUnit` is machine-created, not human-created.
- ‚úÖ Intel + Marvell VSPs + Marvell CP-agent confirmed ‚Äî plus a **third in-tree vendor you didn't mention: Intel Netsec Accelerator** (`intel-netsec`).
- ‚úÖ `ServiceFunctionChain` + `dpu-cni` confirmed (real logic is daemon-side; the manager-side controller is a no-op stub).
- ‚öÝÔ∏è **1c/2c topology is a *deploy-time* choice** (one vs two kubeconfigs via Taskfile targets), not a CR field or code switch.
- ‚öÝÔ∏è **NRI mutating webhook is default-OFF on vanilla Kubernetes** (deploys only on OpenShift/MicroShift or with cert-manager).

**NVIDIA DPF** ‚Äî your summary is accurate; three refinements:

- ‚öÝÔ∏è `DPFOperatorConfig` singleton is **controller-enforced, not webhook-enforced**, and the CR is **Namespaced** (not cluster-scoped).
- ‚úÖ Host/DPU split, Kamaji **and** `type: static` BYO-cluster-via-kubeconfig-Secret, all provisioning + service objects, `DPUDeployment`, and `dpfctl` confirmed.
- ‚ûï New since your notes: **`DPUFlavorTemplate`** (Go-templated flavor rendered per-DPU from `DPUDevice.spec.values`) ‚Äî that's literally the latest commit. Also `DPUDevice`, `DPUNode`, `DPUDiscovery`, and whole `vpc.`/`storage.`/`noderesources.` groups exist beyond your three.

## 3. Every OPI vendor-extension point

The VSP gRPC contract is **not** the only seam ‚Äî but there is **no runtime/out-of-tree plugin path**. Adding a vendor is **5 compile/build-time seams**:

1. **VSP gRPC server** ‚Äî 8 RPCs across `LifeCycleService`, `DeviceService`, `BridgePortService`, `NetworkFunctionService`, `DpuNetworkConfigService`, on unix socket `/var/run/dpu-daemon/vendor-plugin/vendor-plugin.sock`. Daemon client = the `VendorPlugin` Go interface.
2. **`VendorDetector`** added to a **hardcoded Go slice** (`internal/platform/vendordetector.go` ‚Äî literally `// add more detectors here`). **Vendor is selected by PCI autodetection** (vendor/device IDs), not config/nodeSelector. NVIDIA/Mellanox `15b3` is absent today.
3. **Bindata VSP pod template** (`go:embed`'d).
4. **Image env key** in `images.go` + `manager.yaml` ‚Äî the image is an **operator env var, not a CR field** (`DpuOperatorConfigSpec` only has `LogLevel`).
5. **Per-vendor Dockerfile + build target.**

No new CRD is needed to add a vendor; webhooks are generic, not vendor seams.

## 4. Every DPF surface designed for external (machine) driving

1. **`DPUDeployment`** ‚Äî the "single object" API meant to be created programmatically; fans out to DPUSets + DPUServices + chaining it owns via labels.
2. **`DPUCluster type: static` + `spec.kubeconfig` Secret** ‚Äî the **bring-your-own DPU cluster** hook (key `super-admin.conf`; needs `DPFOperatorConfig.spec.staticClusterManager`). This maps cleanly onto OPI's 2-cluster DPU-side kubeconfig.
3. **Custom DPUCluster manager** (ISV `type` prefix) ‚Äî documented "another controller owns the cluster" contract.
4. **`DPUServiceCredentialRequest`** ‚Äî cross-cluster credential issuance (SA + kubeconfig/token Secret; status exposes expiry for rotation).
5. **`DPUService` / `DPUServiceTemplate` / `DPUServiceConfiguration`** ‚Äî Helm-via-ArgoCD delivery + the ISV packaging contract.
6. **Provisioning inputs** (BFB, DPUFlavor(Template), DPUSet) created programmatically.
7. **NodeEffect gates** ‚Äî `Hold` (`wait-for-external-nodeeffect` annotation blocks until cleared) and `CustomAction` let an external orchestrator arbitrate host disruption.
8. **Watchable status** on DPU (25-phase), DPUCluster, DPUService, DPUDeployment, BFB, DPUSet.

**Key friction for the design turn:** DPF has **no lightweight importable `api/` module** (single root `go.mod`, no generated clientset) ‚Äî importing its types drags in OVS/argo/gRPC. An OPI-side component will likely prefer the **dynamic/unstructured client** against DPF GVKs over a hard Go import.

## 5. Requirements & constraints (466 words ‚Üí full text in [research/01-requirements-constraints.md](research/01-requirements-constraints.md))

**Must preserve (OPI side, no regression):** frozen public API surface (`DpuOperatorConfig` shape, existing CRDs ‚Äî add NVIDIA without schema edits); automatic **PCI-autodetection** vendor selection; the **VSP gRPC contract as the vendor boundary**; **zero change** to Intel/Marvell/Netsec detectors, pods, and builds (NVIDIA is purely additive); both 1c/2c topologies keep working.

**Lean on DPF (don't rebuild):** provisioning (BFB flash ‚Üí DMS ‚Üí host-net daemon ‚Üí reboot ‚Üí kubeadm join); DPU-side control plane (Kamaji **or** `static` BYO-cluster); service delivery + SFC (DPUService/Chain/Interface/NAD/IPAM); one-object orchestration via **`DPUDeployment`**; cross-cluster creds via `DPUServiceCredentialRequest`; observability via status conditions + `dpfctl`.

**Out of scope:** modifying DPF internals or the BFB/firmware stack; AMD; re-implementing provisioning/Kamaji/ArgoCD inside OPI; production hardening (HA, upgrade orchestration, real-hardware e2e, deep RBAC); reconciling the opiproject portability fork with downstream; the bonus Go skeleton need only **compile**.

**Design-around frictions:** DPF's no-lightweight-API-module problem (‚Üí dynamic client); NVIDIA may need a Marvell-`cp-agent`/Intel-`P4`-style extra component to bridge the daemon's VSP RPCs into DPF's declarative world.

---

Ready for the next prompt whenever you want to move toward design. One thing worth deciding before then: **do you want the design to target `openshift/dpu-operator` (active line, has `DpuNetwork`, OpenShift/RHEL-flavored) or the `opiproject` portability fork (vendor-neutral plain-k8s, but lagging)?** My recommendation is openshift/dpu-operator ‚Äî but I'll hold for your call rather than bake it in now.

Now design the integration pattern. Propose at least three candidates for
bridging the OPI DPU operator and NVIDIA's DPF operator. At minimum evaluate:

(a) Adapter / vendor-plugin pattern: a new NVIDIA component that implements
    OPI's existing VSP contract, internally translating into DPF CRDs.
(b) Standalone CRD-translation sub-operator: a separate controller, independent
    of the VSP mechanism, that watches OPI-level CRDs cluster-wide and manages
    a parallel set of DPF CRDs, reporting status back via owner references or a
    status-mirroring loop.
(c) Embedded sub-operator: OPI's operator directly installs/vendors the DPF
    Operator as a managed dependency and exposes DPF's native CRDs to OPI users
    directly instead of translating them.
(d) A "do nothing, document how to run both operators side by side" baseline ‚Äî
    include this to prove the added complexity of (a)-(c) is actually justified.
(e) Any other pattern you think deserves a seat at the table.

Score each 1-5 (with reasoning, not just a number) against: reuse of DPF's
existing lifecycle logic (the assignment's explicit top priority), consistency
with OPI's current architecture and CRD conventions, operational blast radius
if the pattern breaks, coupling to DPF's release cadence / API version-skew
risk, the security boundary between the host cluster and the DPU-side
cluster's credentials, multi-tenancy and RBAC implications, and implementation
effort for a first version.

Lay this out as a comparison table. Recommend one pattern as the primary
design and explain in a short paragraph why the runner-up loses. If you
recommend (a), explicitly address whether a second, independent CRD-watching
controller (b) would have been simpler, and why you didn't go that way ‚Äî I
want the reasoning, not just the conclusion.

Design analysis below (also saved to [research/02-pattern-evaluation.md](research/02-pattern-evaluation.md)). No code yet.

## The one fact that drives the whole decision

There's an **impedance mismatch** between the two extension surfaces:

- **OPI's VSP contract is node-scoped, imperative, gRPC.** The VSP is a *per-DPU pod* the daemon calls with imperative RPCs (`Init`, `SetNumVfs`, `CreateBridgePort`, `CreateNetworkFunction`, `SetDpuNetworkConfig`). It assumes the DPU is *already provisioned and reachable* ‚Äî a **Day-2 dataplane** agent.
- **DPF is cluster-scoped, declarative, CRD-driven.** Its crown jewel is **Day-0/1 provisioning + cluster lifecycle** (flash BFB ‚Üí reboot ‚Üí host-net daemon ‚Üí kubeadm-join into a DPU cluster), driven by *cluster-level* controllers reconciling CRDs.

They meet at different layers and different scopes. So the real question every pattern must answer is: **where does the cluster-scoped, DPF-facing orchestration state live, and how does a per-node VSP contract reach it without holding DPU-cluster credentials on every node?**

## Candidates

- **(a) VSP adapter** ‚Äî an NVIDIA VSP implementing the 8 RPCs that *itself* creates DPF CRDs. Native to OPI, but a per-node pod now owns cluster-scoped DPF objects and needs DPU-cluster creds on every node.
- **(b) Standalone translation sub-operator** ‚Äî a separate cluster-scoped controller watching OPI CRDs, owning parallel DPF CRDs, mirroring status. Matches DPF's shape; bypasses OPI's vendor convention and hits a detection chicken-and-egg.
- **(c) Embedded sub-operator** ‚Äî OPI vendors DPF and exposes DPF's native CRDs. Max literal reuse; forks the UX and pins OPI to DPF's release train.
- **(d) Do-nothing / side-by-side** ‚Äî the control case.
- **(e) VSP-fronted translation controller (my hybrid)** ‚Äî an (a)-shaped **thin VSP + detector** at the OPI edge (NVIDIA becomes a first-class autodetected vendor; the daemon handshake is satisfied), backed by a (b)-shaped **singleton cluster controller** that owns the DPF CRDs and holds DPU-cluster creds in *one* place. The VSP carries no cluster creds ‚Äî it signals intent and relays DPF status back through its RPC replies.

## Scoring (1‚Äì5, **5 = most favorable in every column**)

| Criterion (5 = best) | (a) VSP adapter | (b) Translation sub-op | (c) Embedded | (d) Side-by-side | **(e) VSP-fronted ctrl** |
|---|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Reuse of DPF lifecycle *(top priority)* | 3 | 5 | 5 | 5* | **5** |
| Consistency w/ OPI arch & CRD conventions | 5 | 3 | 1 | 1 | **5** |
| Operational blast radius (small = better) | 3 | 4 | 2 | 5 | **4** |
| DPF cadence coupling / version-skew (loose = better) | 3 | 4 | 1 | 5 | **4** |
| Host‚ÜîDPU credential security boundary | 2 | 5 | 3 | 3 | **5** |
| Multi-tenancy & RBAC cleanliness | 2 | 4 | 2 | 3 | **4** |
| Implementation effort v1 (less = better) | 3 | 3 | 4 | 5 | **2** |
| **Total (unweighted)** | **21** | **28** | **18** | **27** | **29** |

\* (d) "reuses" DPF fully only by *not integrating it at all*.

**Rationale for the non-obvious cells:**
- **(a) reuse=3 / security=2 / rbac=2:** a per-node VSP racing to own DPF's cluster singletons (`DPUDeployment`, DPU cluster) is a scope mismatch that pushes you to under-drive DPF or duplicate its orchestration ‚Äî and it smears DPU-cluster kubeconfigs across every DPU node. Consistency=5 because it *is* the designed extension point.
- **(b) reuse=5 / consistency=3:** cluster‚Üîcluster is the honest match; creds live in one controller (security=5). Loses because it sidesteps OPI's VSP/detector convention and the daemon flow: OPI only emits a `DataProcessingUnit` *after* PCI-autodetection via a VSP detector, then expects a VSP gRPC endpoint ‚Äî pure (b) satisfies neither without re-implementing discovery.
- **(c) consistency=1 / coupling=1:** exposing DPF's native CRDs forks the very ecosystem the assignment unifies; vendoring the DPF binary makes every DPF bump an OPI release.
- **(d) reuse=5\* / consistency=1:** top marks on isolation/coupling/effort *because it opts out of the task* ‚Äî the control, not a contender.
- **(e) dominates (b) cell-by-cell except effort:** it's (b)'s engine + a thin (a) shim that closes (b)'s two gaps (native detection + handshake) for a small cost (consistency 3‚Üí5), keeping creds in the single controller. Price = most moving parts ‚Üí effort=2.

**Read the table with the goal in mind:** these seven criteria don't include "delivers a *unified* OPI ecosystem," which is why (d) looks deceptively strong. Weight *DPF reuse* (stated top priority) and *OPI consistency*, and (e) leads, with (b) the true runner-up.

## Recommendation: (e) ‚Äî VSP-fronted translation controller

`thin NVIDIA VSP + VendorDetector (PCI 15b3/BlueField)` at the OPI edge ‚Üí signals a **singleton `dpuf-adapter` controller** in the host cluster ‚Üí controller reconciles OPI intent into DPF CRDs (one `DPUDeployment` per DPU set; `DPUServiceChain`/`Interface` for OPI `ServiceFunctionChain`/`DpuNetwork`) ‚Üí DPF does provisioning + services ‚Üí controller **mirrors DPF status back** onto OPI `DataProcessingUnit`/`ServiceFunctionChain`, which the VSP surfaces via its RPC replies. DPU-cluster creds are obtained once, in the controller, through DPF's `DPUServiceCredentialRequest` / static-`DPUCluster` kubeconfig Secret ‚Äî **never on a node**.

**To OPI this simply *is* pattern (a):** the external contract is exactly the VSP gRPC + detector, NVIDIA looks like any other vendor, and the OPI CRD surface is untouched. The only change from na√Øve (a) is *hoisting the DPF-facing orchestration out of the node pod into a cluster singleton* ‚Äî which is what flips security, RBAC, and DPF-reuse from mediocre to strong.

**Why the runner-up (b) loses.** Pure (b) is genuinely tempting and, in raw LOC, **simpler** ‚Äî one controller, no gRPC server, no detector, no node pod (28 vs 29). It loses for two concrete reasons. *First, the OPI entry point:* OPI surfaces a DPU only after its daemon PCI-autodetects it via a registered `VendorDetector` and dials the VSP socket ‚Äî with no NVIDIA detector/VSP, the hardware is invisible to OPI and the daemon's node flow stalls, so (b) must re-implement discovery OPI already does and still leaves the handshake unsatisfied. *Second, convention/isolation:* a side controller watching OPI CRDs is "an operator bolted next to OPI," not "NVIDIA, a supported OPI vendor" ‚Äî it diverges from how Intel/Marvell/Netsec plug in. (e) keeps the DPF-shaped code exactly where (b) puts it, but reaches it through OPI's front door, and the shim that buys that is genuinely thin.

## The a-vs-b reasoning, head-on (as you asked)

(a) and (e) share the same OPI-facing contract, so "recommend (a)" and "recommend (e)" are the *same answer at the seam* ‚Äî the difference is **where cluster state lives**. Pure (a) puts DPF orchestration *and* DPU-cluster credentials inside the per-node VSP pod: it fights DPF's cluster-scoped model (racing singletons, N-way ownership of `DPUDeployment`) and spreads privileged kubeconfigs across every DPU node (security=2, rbac=2). Would a single independent controller (b) have avoided that? **Yes ‚Äî which is exactly why (e) adopts (b)'s controller as its engine.** So I didn't "not go with (b)"; I absorbed it. The full reasoning: **take (a)'s boundary (OPI-native, first-class vendor) and (b)'s engine (cluster-scoped DPF reuse, one credential holder), and reject both pure forms** ‚Äî (a) for the node-level credential/scope mismatch, (b) for the missing OPI front door.

---

Three decisions I'd want to settle before detailed design (noted in the file):
1. **Provisioning ownership** ‚Äî OPI's 2-cluster DPU-side cluster becomes DPF's via `DPUCluster type: static` (OPI supplies the kubeconfig Secret) vs. DPF/Kamaji owning it. *Leaning static.*
2. **VSP‚Üícontroller signal channel** ‚Äî reuse the existing `DataProcessingUnit` CR vs. a new namespaced request CR. *Leaning reuse, to add no OPI API surface.*
3. **DPF client strategy** ‚Äî dynamic/unstructured client against DPF GVKs vs. vendoring DPF's heavy Go module. *Leaning dynamic client.*

Want me to lock those three (I can pose them as explicit choices), or move straight into the detailed architecture + sequence diagrams for pattern (e)?

Write the full architecture for the pattern you recommended. This becomes
architecture_design.md, so write it as a finished document ‚Äî proper headings,
no filler, no "as an AI..." framing. Include:

1. A one-paragraph executive summary.
2. A component diagram (Mermaid graph/flowchart) showing: OPI's existing
   controller-manager, the per-node dpu-daemon, the existing Intel/Marvell VSPs
   (for context), your new NVIDIA component, the DPF operator and its
   controllers, and the Host Cluster / DPU Cluster split. Label every edge with
   what crosses it (a CR, a gRPC call, a kubeconfig/Secret, etc.).
3. A CRD/API mapping table: every OPI-level concept a user or the existing
   daemon touches, mapped to the DPF CRD(s) your component creates or watches
   on its behalf (e.g. OPI's ServiceFunctionChain ‚Üí DPF's DPUServiceChain +
   DPUServiceInterface). Call out anywhere the mapping isn't 1:1 and how you
   close the gap.
4. At least three Mermaid sequenceDiagrams: (i) first-time onboarding of a new
   BlueField node, from node labeling through the DPU joining its DPU Cluster
   as a node; (ii) a user creating an OPI-level workload/service-chain intent
   and it landing as a running DPUService/DPUServiceChain; (iii) a failure or
   day-2 scenario of your choice (DPU Cluster temporarily unreachable, or a DPF
   version upgrade) showing how your adapter surfaces the problem at the OPI
   layer instead of failing silently.
5. A status/condition propagation design: how DPF's Ready/condition semantics
   on DPU, DPUService, etc. get reflected into whatever status the OPI-level
   objects expose, so `kubectl get` at the OPI layer stays truthful without the
   user needing to know DPF exists underneath.
6. A security/RBAC section: what credentials your component needs against the
   DPU Cluster, how they're scoped and stored ‚Äî compare this to how DPF's own
   "static" DPUCluster mode already consumes a kubeconfig Secret, and how you'd
   avoid over-broad permissions.
7. A trade-off analysis section (refine what you produced last turn into
   finished-document form).
8. An "Assumptions / Out of scope" section listing every call you made where
   the assignment was ambiguous (vendor choice, DPF version targeted, cluster
   topology, etc.) and why.

Output the full Markdown now.

`architecture_design.md` is written to the repo root ‚Äî a finished, self-contained document built directly on the pattern we selected last turn (**(e), the VSP-fronted translation controller**: thin NVIDIA VSP + PCI detector at the OPI edge, backed by a singleton `dpuf-adapter` controller owning all DPF CRDs and the sole DPU-cluster credential).

**What's in it, mapped to your 8 requirements:**

1. **Executive summary** ‚Äî one paragraph framing the impedance mismatch (node-scoped/imperative OPI vs. cluster-scoped/declarative DPF) and how the adapter absorbs it.
2. **Component diagram** ‚Äî Mermaid flowchart with Host Cluster / DPU Cluster split, OPI controller-manager, per-node `dpu-daemon`, the new NVIDIA VSP, Intel/Marvell VSPs for context, the `dpuf-adapter`, DPF operator + provisioning/service controllers. Every edge labeled, plus a payload legend table (CRs, the 8 gRPC RPCs, the kubeconfig Secret, watches, etc.).
3. **CRD/API mapping table** ‚Äî every OPI concept ‚Üí DPF object(s), with a **1:1? column** that explicitly flags the non-1:1 rows (`ServiceFunctionChain` ‚Üí `DPUServiceChain` + `DPUServiceInterface` + `DPUService`; `DataProcessingUnit` ‚Üí `DPUSet`/`DPU`/`DPUFlavor`; the `Init`-endpoint timing gap) and how each is closed.
4. **Three sequenceDiagrams** ‚Äî (i) BlueField onboarding from node label through `kubeadm`-join, (ii) `ServiceFunctionChain` intent ‚Üí running `DPUServiceChain`, (iii) DPU Cluster unreachable, showing the `Degraded/DPUClusterUnreachable` condition surfacing at the OPI layer (with the DPF-upgrade variant noted).
5. **Status/condition propagation** ‚Äî the DPF‚ÜíOPI‚ÜíVSP-reply upward flow, a least-ready aggregation rule with a mapping table, and the no-silent-success / no-stale-green guarantees.
6. **Security/RBAC** ‚Äî exactly what the adapter needs, compared head-on to DPF's own static `DPUCluster` kubeconfig-Secret pattern, and why node pods hold zero DPU-cluster creds.
7. **Trade-off analysis** ‚Äî the scoring table refined into finished form, with the (a)/(b) "why not" arguments and residual-risk mitigations.
8. **Assumptions / out of scope** ‚Äî 9 numbered assumptions (NVIDIA, `openshift/dpu-operator`, DPF version pinning, 2-cluster static topology, `DataProcessingUnit` as the signal channel, dynamic client, etc.) plus the explicit out-of-scope list.

**Real dependencies ‚Äî no stand-ins.** I probed first: the network resolves, and `sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime` + `k8s.io/apimachinery` build fine here. Those are real imports used throughout (`ctrl.Request`, `ctrl.Result`, `client.Client`, server-side apply, `meta.SetStatusCondition`, `unstructured`, `logr`). ## What I built (Kubebuilder-style layout)

```
go.mod                                    module github.com/opiproject/opi-nvidia-dpf-adapter
api/opi/v1/                               mirrored OPI CRD types + hand-written deepcopy
  ‚îú‚îÄ groupversion_info.go                 (config.openshift.io/v1) + the reuse-vs-new-CRD rationale
  ‚îú‚îÄ servicefunctionchain_types.go
  ‚îî‚îÄ dataprocessingunit_types.go
internal/dpf/types.go                     local mirror structs + GVKs for DPF CRDs (dynamic-client route)
internal/translate/translate.go           Translator: OPI intent ‚Üí DPF unstructured objects (¬ß3 mapping)
internal/vsp/vendorplugin.go              8-RPC VendorPlugin interface (mirrors OPI's real VSP contract)
internal/vsp/nvidia_vsp.go                NvidiaVSP: thin, holds no DPU-cluster creds

internal/controller/status.go             DPF phase/condition ‚Üí OPI condition mapping (¬ß5)
```

Mapping to your five requirements:

1. **CRD types** ‚Äî I chose to **reuse the existing OPI CRDs** (`ServiceFunctionChain`, `DataProcessingUnit`) rather than invent an adapter-owned CR, with the reasoning in a header comment on `groupversion_info.go`: "maximizing reuse" means NVIDIA users write the *same* CRs as Intel/Marvell users, so the adapter's OPI-facing objects are the real ones. Types are local mirrors (comment names the upstream source + group/version), with hand-written deepcopy standing in for `controller-gen`.
2. **VendorPlugin interface + concrete type** ‚Äî the interface mirrors OPI's real 8 RPCs across 5 gRPC services; `NvidiaVSP` implements all of them, with a compile-time `var _ VendorPlugin = (*NvidiaVSP)(nil)` assertion.
3. **Translation layer** ‚Äî `Translator` interface + `DPFTranslator`, turning one `ServiceFunctionChain` into `DPUServiceChain` + `DPUServiceInterface`(s) + `DPUService`(s), and a `DataProcessingUnit` into a `DPUDeployment`, exactly per the arch mapping table. DPF types are local mirrors, each labeled with its real CRD/group/version ‚Äî no heavy `doca-platform` import.
4. **Reconciler** ‚Äî two `Reconcile(ctx, req) (ctrl.Result, error)` loops showing the full cycle: fetch OPI object ‚Üí DPU-cluster reachability guard ‚Üí translate ‚Üí server-side apply the DPF objects ‚Üí read status back ‚Üí aggregate least-ready ‚Üí patch OPI `.status` conditions ‚Üí requeue (fast poll while provisioning, backoff on transient DPU-cluster loss).
5. **Real error handling + structured logging** ‚Äî `logr` throughout (zero `fmt.Println`), wrapped errors with `%w`, a `errDPUClusterUnreachable` classifier that surfaces `Degraded` instead of failing silently (the ¬ß4-iii scenario), and `TODO(dpuf)` markers where a production version would add the DPF served-version preflight and the unstructured watch sources ‚Äî so it reads as paused-mid-implementation, not a toy.

Before we finalize, critique your own design like a skeptical OPI maintainer
reviewing this as a pull request:

- Where does this design silently reimplement something DPF already does well
  (the assignment's core ask is to avoid exactly that)?
- What happens on a DPF minor-version upgrade that renames or restructures a
  CRD you depend on ‚Äî does your adapter fail loudly, or silently drift?
- What's the blast radius if your new NVIDIA component has a bug ‚Äî can it
  affect the existing Intel/Marvell paths at all, and if so, how do you
  isolate it?
- Is there a multi-tenancy problem if two DPUClusters, or two independent
  fleets of BlueField DPUs, need separate lifecycles?
- How brittle is the coupling if OPI's own operator changes its VSP contract
  in the future?
- Does anything in the Go skeleton need to change based on this critique?

Done. I reviewed the design as a skeptical OPI maintainer would, and the critique drew blood ‚Äî three of the six questions exposed **real defects**, which I fixed in both `architecture_design.md` (new **¬ß9**) and the Go skeleton. Build/vet/gofmt stay clean.

## The findings, honestly ranked

**Q3 ‚Äî blast radius (the worst one).** `ServiceFunctionChain` and `DataProcessingUnit` are *vendor-neutral* OPI CRDs ‚Äî every vendor shares the same GVK. My first cut watched them cluster-wide with **no vendor filter**, so my NVIDIA adapter would happily translate an *Intel* service-chain into DPF objects and corrupt it. That's not hypothetical; it's what the code did. **Fixed** with defense-in-depth: an informer predicate (`nvidiaPredicate`) drops non-NVIDIA events, and a reconcile-time `ClusterResolver.Resolve` guard exits without writing status for anything not ours (re-checked at act-time because labels can change between event and reconcile). New file `internal/controller/fleet.go`.

**Q1 ‚Äî silently reimplementing DPF.** The translator minted **one `DPUDeployment` per node** ‚Äî but DPF's `DPUSet` already fans a single deployment across every node its selector matches. Per-node deployments reimplement exactly that grouping. **Fixed:** `DeploymentForFleet` now emits one fleet-level deployment and lets DPF fan out. (I also checked the near-no-op VSP RPCs and the status mirror and left them ‚Äî they're intent-recording and projection, not parallel logic.)

**Q2 ‚Äî DPF version drift.** A renamed/removed DPF CRD surfaced as a generic apply error ‚Äî quasi-silent drift. **Fixed:** `classifyDPFError` catches the exact no-match shape (`meta.IsNoMatchError` / `runtime.IsNotRegisteredError`) ‚Üí `errDPFVersionUnsupported` ‚Üí `DPFReady=False, reason=DPFVersionUnsupported`, and the adapter refuses to proceed. Loud, specific, at the OPI layer. (A startup RESTMapper pre-flight remains a `TODO(dpuf)` ‚Äî earlier signal, not a correctness gap.)

**Q4 ‚Äî multi-tenancy.** Single hard-coded namespace + one probe meant two fleets would collide on names and share a credential. **Fixed:** a `Fleet` bundles per-fleet namespace/selector/flavor/**credential-probe**, object names are fleet-prefixed, and `SingleFleetResolver` swaps to a multi-fleet resolver drop-in.

**Plus a condition-ownership bug** surfaced while wiring status: I was writing the top-level `Ready` that OPI's own daemon also manages ‚Üí two writers flapping. Renamed the adapter's condition to **`DPFReady`** (single-writer rule).

## What I deliberately did *not* change (stated, not dropped)

- **Q5 ‚Äî VSP contract brittleness:** I declined to add a versioned internal VSP facade. Additive contract changes are already cheap (thin interface, localized `nvidia_vsp.go`); a *breaking semantic* redefinition needs rework no shim avoids, and OPI's contract has been stable across three vendors. The real mitigation: the mirrored interface turns a break into a **compile error** the day we drop the mirror ‚Äî loud, not latent. Adding indirection now to hedge a hypothetical break wasn't worth it.
- **Singleton adapter (no HA):** still v1-out-of-scope; a crash yields stale-but-truthful status, not corruption.
- **Dynamic client over hard DPF import:** *reaffirmed and strengthened* ‚Äî the Q2 drift detection depends on catching no-match at runtime, which the dynamic client gives you.

## Every assumption made across the whole exercise, in one place

**Scope / target choices**
1. **Vendor = NVIDIA** (assignment allowed NVIDIA *or* AMD) ‚Äî DPF is the mature reuse target.
2. **Target repo = `openshift/dpu-operator`** (the active line), not `opiproject/dpu-operator` (a lagging portability fork). Both `PROJECT` files declare the openshift repo anyway.
3. **BlueField-3-class hardware**; DOCA/BFB stack supplied out-of-band; PCI detection via vendor `15b3` + BlueField device IDs.

**API / integration facts I treated as ground truth**
4. **OPI CRDs live under `config.openshift.io/v1`** (`ServiceFunctionChain`, `DataProcessingUnit`, `DpuOperatorConfig`, ‚Ä¶) ‚Äî mirrored locally rather than imported.
5. **DPF groups/versions** as of `doca-platform` main (2026-07-03): `provisioning.dpu.nvidia.com`, `svc.dpu.nvidia.com`, `operator.dpu.nvidia.com`, all `v1alpha1` ‚Äî **pinned, an integration-time decision**, not baked into OPI. A served-version mismatch is surfaced as `DPFVersionUnsupported`.
6. **`DataProcessingUnit` is created by the daemon** post-autodetect (not by humans, not by the adapter); the adapter only writes its status.
7. **DPF is driven via the dynamic/unstructured client**, not a hard Go import (DPF ships no lightweight `api/` module).

**Design decisions where the assignment was silent**
8. **Reuse existing OPI CRDs; add no new adapter CRD** ‚Äî the signal channel is the existing `DataProcessingUnit` (status + annotation).
9. **2-cluster topology with `DPUCluster type: static`** ‚Äî OPI supplies the DPU-cluster kubeconfig; 1-cluster supported but not primary.
10. **`DPUFlavor`/BFB authored out-of-band by an admin**; the adapter references a default, doesn't generate firmware config.
11. **A vendor label `dpu.openshift.io/vendor=nvidia`** must be stamped by the NVIDIA detector/VSP ‚Äî this is the isolation key (fallback: resolve vendor by PCI id at reconcile time, noted as heavier/racier).
12. **Adapter owns a distinct `DPFReady` condition**, never the daemon's top-level `Ready` (single-writer rule).
13. **One fleet-level `DPUDeployment`** (not per-node) ‚Äî DPF's `DPUSet` does the fan-out.
14. **Single-fleet default wiring** (`SingleFleetResolver`); multi-fleet is a drop-in resolver swap.

**Skeleton-fidelity assumptions (bonus is "must compile," not production)**
15. **Controller-runtime v0.17.3 / apimachinery v0.29.2, pinned** ‚Äî newer releases need Go 1.26; local Go is 1.25.4.
16. **OPI/DPF types are local field-subset mirrors** with hand-written deepcopy (stands in for `controller-gen`); each names its real source.
17. **The VSP contract is mirrored as a Go interface**, not imported from OPI's generated protobuf ‚Äî so a *breaking* OPI VSP-contract change would surface at the eventual real import, not in this skeleton.
18. **`TODO(dpuf)` markers** flag deliberately-unfinished spots (DPF unstructured watches, RESTMapper version pre-flight, real DPU-name lookup by ownerRef, HA/leader-election) ‚Äî real, not gaps in the design.

## What was WRONG and is now fixed

**1. `DataProcessingUnit` status is `conditions`-only ‚Äî no `phase`, no endpoint field.** The real upstream shape (`api/v1`, verified 2026-07-03) is spec `{dpuProductName, isDpuSide, nodeName}`, status = conditions, `scope=Cluster`, `shortName=dpu`, and printer columns `DPU Product / DPU Side / Node Name / Status`. My mirror had invented a `status.phase` and a `status.dataplaneEndpoint` ‚Äî both deleted. The datapath endpoint now rides an **annotation** (`EndpointAnnotation`), since the real status has nowhere to put it, and the VSP intent likewise travels as an annotation. Fixed in `api/opi/v1/dataprocessingunit_types.go`.
**2. Vendor scoping had an invented label.** I'd keyed on `dpu.openshift.io/vendor`. The real CR already carries the vendor in `spec.dpuProductName` (the `DPU Product` column), so DPUs need **no** label ‚Äî the adapter matches the product string. Only `ServiceFunctionChain` (no product field) needs a hint, and for it I use the **shipping** `dpu.config.openshift.io/vendor` domain (matching the real `dpu.config.openshift.io/dpuside` selector), not an invented one. Fixed in `fleet.go` and ¬ß8/¬ß9.
**3. `ServiceFunctionChain` gained its real `spec.nodeSelector`** and confirmed `NetworkFunction={name,image}`, `shortName=sfc`.

## What HELD UP under the real sources

- **`opi-nvidia-bridge` is exactly what we assumed** ‚Äî a runtime OPI Storage gRPC bridge (NVMe/VirtioBlk emulation over SNAP), a data-plane component, not an operator. Confirms storage is a separate plane; boundary to be formalized in a dedicated section.
- **Red Hat's 4.20 multivendor writeup ships Intel Netsec, Intel IPU E2100, and Marvell** side-by-side in `oc get dpu` through the same VSP boundary ‚Äî and NVIDIA is absent. That's external validation of pattern (e) AND our concrete target (the NVIDIA row). Woven into ¬ß7.
- **DPF's storage subsystem is real and vendor-pluggable** ‚Äî SNAP Controller + Vendor CSI selected by `StorageVendor`/`StoragePolicy`. Same plugin-behind-a-CR instinct as OPI's VSP; cited in ¬ß7 as independent precedent.

## ¬ß10 ‚Äî Storage boundary

Framed as three planes with a one-word relation each, because "out of scope" is only credible if the line is precise ‚Äî DPUs are exactly where network and storage offload share silicon:
- **A. `opi-nvidia-bridge`** (runtime OPI Storage gRPC ‚Äî NVMe/VirtioBlk emulation over SNAP): **ignored**. It's a data-plane gRPC server with no cluster creds and no CRDs; this adapter never issues an OPI Storage RPC.
- **B. DPF's SNAP/`StorageVendor` subsystem** (SNAP Controller + Vendor CSI, selected by `StorageVendor`/`StoragePolicy`): **ignored now, cleanly invokable later** ‚Äî if storage were pulled in scope, the adapter would emit those CRDs (or wrap the bridge in a `DPUService`) through the *same* dynamic client and fleet model. Not taken in v1.
- **C. This adapter** (network + lifecycle): the disjoint owner.

Plus a 3-axis collision analysis, all clear: **CRD ownership** (owned GVK set is disjoint from `StorageVendor`/`StoragePolicy` ‚Äî zero shared objects), **RBAC** (no verb on storage CRDs ‚Äî an accidental write fails closed), and **data path** (adapter never opens the bridge's SNAP socket). The planes share *hardware*, never *control*.

## ¬ß11 ‚Äî Definition of Done

The single observable acceptance test: an NVIDIA BlueField-3 row in `oc get dpu`, reaching `Status=True` through the same VSP boundary, with no new OPI CRD and no change to another vendor's path. The table uses the real five columns and shows the NVIDIA rows below the three Red-Hat-shipping vendors:

```
NAME                    DPU PRODUCT          DPU SIDE   NODE NAME   STATUS
worker-3-marvell-dpu    Marvell CN10K        true       worker-3    True
worker-4-bf3-host       NVIDIA BlueField-3   false      worker-4    True
worker-4-bf3-dpu        NVIDIA BlueField-3   true       worker-4    True
```

`DPU Side` is the real boolean, so each physical DPU is a host-side `false` row + a dpu-side `true` row. Backed by a done checklist where every item traces to a section, and HA/upgrade/e2e-on-hardware are honestly marked not-claimed (¬ß8 out-of-scope).

- DPF deploys DPUServices via its own internal ArgoCD instance and manages disruptive updates through a maintenance-operator that handles node draining. Two of the four questions surfaced real bugs; both are fixed in code with tests, and the review is written up as a new **¬ß12** that parallels ¬ß9. All four are answered "state it, decide, say why" ‚Äî nothing left hanging.

## The two real bugs (fixed)

**Q4 ‚Äî the created DPF objects were orphans.** The first cut stamped only *labels* on the `DPUServiceChain`/`Interface`/`Service` objects ‚Äî no `ownerReference`, no finalizer. Deleting the OPI `ServiceFunctionChain` left them behind forever, and DPF's internal ArgoCD would keep the orphaned NF workloads *running*. An ownerReference is the wrong fix (they'd be cross-namespace ‚Üí invalid ‚Üí risk premature GC), so the adapter now owns cleanup via a **finalizer** (`dpu.nvidia.com/chain-cleanup`): added before any DPF object is created, cascade-deletes the fleet-scoped objects on SFC deletion, never drops the finalizer on a failed delete, and stays attribution-safe (never deletes objects it can't prove are NVIDIA's). Proven by `TestServiceFunctionChainReconciler_FinalizerGarbageCollectsDPFObjects`.

**Q3 ‚Äî readiness-signal drift was silent.** The adapter reads `DPU.status.phase`; `NestedString` returns `""` for a *missing* field, and `mapDPUPhase("")` folds that into `Provisioning`. So if DPF moved the readiness signal (it has evolved before), a ready DPU would read as **eternally "Provisioning"** with no hint why ‚Äî ¬ß9-Q2's GVK classifier never fires because the CRD didn't move, only the status *shape* did. Fixed: `readDPUPhase` now distinguishes "no status yet" (legit early window ‚Üí Provisioning) from "populated status, no phase" (drift ‚Üí new sentinel `errDPFStatusSchemaUnknown`), surfaced loudly as `DPFReady=False, reason=DPFStatusUnrecognized`. Proven by `TestDataProcessingUnitReconciler_ReadinessSignalDriftIsLoud`.

## The two that were already right (now pinned / stated)

**Q1 ‚Äî no fight with ArgoCD or the maintenance-operator.** The adapter is a *requester* of `DPUService`, never a co-reconciler of the Argo release. For the maintenance-operator: SSA `ForceOwnership` only reclaims fields the *applied body* names, and the translator emits a minimal body (dpuFlavor + nodeSelector; image/refs) that never touches status or any pause/maintenance field ‚Äî so no field-ownership collision, and the adapter has no node-drain / DPU-delete path, leaving disruptive-update sequencing 100% DPF's. This constraint is now documented on both `apply()` methods so it can't be quietly broadened. Declined (stated): surfacing a dedicated `UnderMaintenance` reason ‚Äî it would couple to the maintenance-operator's CRD.

**Q2 ‚Äî Host Trusted, now explicit.** The design implicitly assumed Host Trusted; ¬ß8-assumption 13 now says so and explains it *must* be ‚Äî the adapter runs host-side and holds the DPU-cluster kubeconfig there, which Zero Trust forbids. It targets Host Trusted because OPI's onboarding seam is itself host-side; integrating at OPI's actual seam *is* a Host-Trusted operation. The credential-free node pods are host-trust hardening, not Zero Trust, and the doc no longer lets that blur.

Verified against upstream ‚Äî and the enumeration I'd just made prominent was **wrong**, so this was a real correction, not cosmetic. I pulled `openshift/dpu-operator:dpu-api/api.proto` via `gh` and read the full service set.

## What the real proto says

**7 RPCs across 5 services** (not 8):
- `LifeCycleService.Init`
- `DeviceService.{GetDevices, SetNumVfs}`
- `NetworkFunctionService.{CreateNetworkFunction, DeleteNetworkFunction}`
- `DpuNetworkConfigService.SetDpuNetworkConfig`
- `HeartbeatService.Ping`

Two concrete errors in my skeleton mirror + doc: (1) I had invented a `BridgePortService` (`CreateBridgePort`/`DeleteBridgePort`) that **does not exist** ‚Äî bridge wiring actually rides in `NFRequest.bridge_id` on `CreateNetworkFunction`; (2) I had **omitted** the real `HeartbeatService.Ping` (which, fittingly, is the "health RPC" the ¬ß4-iii failure diagram was already gesturing at).

## Fixed in code (the doc claims doc‚Üîcode consistency, so both had to move)

- `internal/vsp/vendorplugin.go` ‚Äî rewrote the `VendorPlugin` mirror: dropped `BridgePort` (struct + 2 methods), added `HeartbeatService.Ping` with `PingRequest`/`PingResponse`, corrected `NetworkFunction` to the real `{Input, Output, BridgeID}` and `DpuNetworkConfig` to `{IsAccelerated}`; header now says "7 RPCs across 5 services" and notes the BridgePort correction (verified via gh, 2026-07-04).
- `internal/vsp/nvidia_vsp.go` ‚Äî removed the two BridgePort methods, updated `CreateNetworkFunction`/`DeleteNetworkFunction`/`SetDpuNetworkConfig` to the corrected shapes, added a `Ping` liveness responder.
- `internal/dpf/types.go` ‚Äî fixed a stale `CreateBridgePort` comment.

## Fixed in the doc

¬ß1 ("seven RPCs across five services"), ¬ß2 hero-diagram edge (`7 gRPC RPCs`), the ¬ß2 edge legend (full service‚ÜíRPC enumeration + explicit "no `BridgePort` RPC, verified against `dpu-api/api.proto`"), the ¬ß3 SFC + VSP-RPC rows, the ¬ß4-ii and ¬ß4-iii sequence diagrams (`Ping()` / `HeartbeatService`), ¬ß9-Q1, and ¬ß7-Q5.
